purpose: §9 Metric 1 — first-pass critical-vector detection recall on known incidents
note: Findings constructed from post-mortem documentation, not live bytecode analysis. Advisory tier only. No precision/recall numbers asserted.

groundTruth.name: Euler Finance
groundTruth.rootCause: donateToReserves() mutates eToken/dToken accounting without a post-call solvency invariant check; deferLiquidityCheck() allows deferred health enforcement inside a flash-loan callback, enabling intentional undercollateralization.
exploitMechanism: Deposit DAI as collateral, borrow eDAI
Call donateToReserves() to inflate dToken.underlyingBalance without health check
Take out full collateral while technically insolvent

vectors.description: No solvency check after donateToReserves(); reserves decrease without enforced health factor
vectors.gap: PARTIAL — BYTECODE_PRIVILEGE repurposed for missing-invariant class; no dedicated surface

vectors.description: deferLiquidityCheck() permits unchecked leveraged position within single tx
vectors.gap: NONE — LP_UNLOCK maps cleanly to flash-loan amplification vectors

groundTruth.stageBEscalationNote: ACCOUNTING counterexamples do NOT trigger hardBlockFromSymbolic. HARDBLOCK for Euler is Stage-A-only. Calibration gap: critical accounting invariant violations have no Stage B escalation path absent a Stage A hardBlock flag.

hardBlockCandidates.description: donateToReserves() mutates eToken balance and dToken.underlyingBalance without a post-call solvency
deferLiquidityCheck(address account, bytes calldata data) callable by any user; defers the solvency

counterexamplesFound.description: Total protocol reserves never decrease without a corresponding authorized withdrawal event
counterexample: donateToReserves(amount=30_000_000e18) called inside deferLiquidityCheck() callback
dToken.underlyingBalance increases by amount; no corresponding authorized withdrawal emitted
Post-call: reserves decreased without withdrawal event — ACCT-001 violated

counterexamplesFound.description: Reserve conservation: opening_balance + deposits == closing_balance + withdrawals
counterexample: Trace: deposit(30M DAI) → borrow(eDAI) → donateToReserves(30M eDAI)
opening_balance + deposits = 30M; closing_balance + withdrawals = 30M + borrow_amount
Conservation violated: attacker exits with collateral while position is insolvent

highSeverityFindings.description: Cross-function reentrancy: deposit() and withdraw() share mutable state
Reserve donation manipulates collateral accounting: donateToReserves() inflates dToken.underlyingBal
Re-initialisation attack: initialize() callable again after upgrade sets owner to attacker
deferLiquidityCheck() has no access control: any external caller can defer solvency enforcement for
uint256 overflow in unchecked accumulation of fee-on-transfer tokens
Classic reentrancy: external call made before state update in withdraw()
Permanent protocol brick via self-destruct of singleton library contract

groundTruth.name: Cream Finance
groundTruth.rootCause: AMP token ERC-777 tokensToSend hook fires before Cream Finance borrow() updates internal balance; re-entrant borrow() call within the hook extracts a second loan using stale (pre-update) balance. MakerDAO flash loan amplifies the attack.
exploitMechanism: Deposit as crDAI collateral in Cream
AMP tokensToSend hook re-enters borrow() with stale balance

vectors.description: ERC-777 tokensToSend hook re-enters borrow() before accounting update (CEI violation)
vectors.gap: NONE — BYTECODE_PRIVILEGE (missing reentrancy guard) and REENTRANCY agent cover this

vectors.description: MakerDAO flash loan enables double-borrow in single tx via reentrancy path

groundTruth.stageBEscalationNote: REENTRANCY_STATE counterexamples do NOT trigger hardBlockFromSymbolic. HARDBLOCK for Cream is Stage-A-only. Same escalation gap as Euler: reentrancy findings need Stage A hardBlock flag to reach HARDBLOCK — Stage B alone is insufficient.

hardBlockCandidates.description: borrow() has no nonReentrant guard; integrates AMP token (ERC-777 compatible) which fires tokensToSe
MakerDAO MCD_FLASH (0x1EB4CF3A948E7D72A198fe073cCb8C7a948cD853) used to obtain ~500M DAI flash loan;

counterexamplesFound.description: No external call is made before state mutation is finalized (checks-effects-interactions)
counterexample: borrow(borrowAmount) calls AMP token transferFrom before zeroing borrower balance
AMP tokensToSend hook fires mid-transfer; attacker contract re-enters borrow()
Re-entrant borrow() observes stale (pre-update) balance — CEI invariant violated

counterexamplesFound.description: A reentrant call cannot produce a different post-state outcome than a non-reentrant call
counterexample: Non-reentrant path: borrow(500M DAI) → balance updated → exit; net extracted = 500M
Reentrant path: borrow(500M DAI) → tokensToSend hook → borrow(500M DAI again) → both settle
Post-state diverges: reentrant path extracts 2× collateral vs. non-reentrant

highSeverityFindings.description: Storage collision between proxy and implementation overwrites owner slot
ERC-777 tokensToSend hook in AMP token triggers re-entry into borrow() before Cream Finance balance
Permanent protocol brick via self-destruct of singleton library contract
Role escalation via misconfigured AccessControl grant path
Flash-loan amplifies reentrancy: 500M DAI deposited as crDAI collateral; ERC-777 hook on borrow() re
Re-initialisation attack: initialize() callable again after upgrade sets owner to attacker
Rounding exploit in partial-fill order book favours attacker per iteration
Flashloan inflates reserve before arithmetic check allows outsized withdrawal
Unguarded state transition allows EMERGENCY_SHUTDOWN to be reversed by anyone
Cross-contract state inconsistency: vault and strategy disagree on deposited amount

groundTruth.name: Nomad Bridge
groundTruth.attackerAddress: (multiple — free-for-all copy-paste exploit after first tx)
groundTruth.rootCause: Routine upgrade initialized Replica.committedRoot to bytes32(0). Since confirmedRoots[0x00] was set true, acceptableRoot() returned true for every message with root=0x00. Any caller could fabricate bridge messages with zero root and execute arbitrary withdrawals without a valid Merkle proof.
exploitMechanism: Upgrade sets committedRoot = bytes32(0) → confirmedRoots[0x00] = true
Attacker submits process(message) with fabricated body and root=0x00
acceptableRoot(0x00) returns true — no Merkle proof required
Bridge executes token transfer; exploit copied by hundreds of addresses

vectors.description: Upgrade sets committedRoot=bytes32(0), auto-confirming zero root for all bridge messages
vectors.gap: NONE — PROXY_ADMIN captures upgrade-introduced state corruption cleanly

vectors.description: process() accepts any message with root=0x00 without Merkle proof
vectors.gap: PARTIAL — BYTECODE_PRIVILEGE used as fallback; no dedicated INPUT_VALIDATION surface

groundTruth.stageBEscalationNote: UPGRADE and AUTHORIZATION counterexamples DO trigger hardBlockFromSymbolic. Nomad is the only protocol where Stage B independently reaches HARDBLOCK, regardless of Stage A findings.

hardBlockCandidates.description: Routine upgrade to Replica.sol initialized committedRoot to bytes32(0); confirmedRoots[0x00] consequ
process() in Replica.sol accepts any message when root=bytes32(0); no null-root guard; unprivileged

counterexamplesFound.description: No execution path exists where an unprivileged caller reaches privileged state
counterexample: process(bytes memory message) called by unprivileged address 0xb5c55f76f90cc528...
require(acceptableRoot(msg.root)) passes for root=bytes32(0)
Bridge executes IERC20.transfer() on behalf of forged message — privileged state reached

counterexamplesFound.description: Implementation address cannot be changed without satisfying the configured timelock
counterexample: Replica.initialize() called during upgrade with committedRoot=bytes32(0)
confirmedRoots[bytes32(0)] set to true as side-effect of zero-root initialization
Implementation change introduces universally-confirmed root — timelock constraint bypassed via init error

counterexamplesFound.description: No upgrade path exists that bypasses the authorization constraint
counterexample: Post-upgrade: any caller submits process(message) with msg.root=bytes32(0)
acceptableRoot(bytes32(0)) returns true — authorization constraint bypassed
Upgrade path introduced a state that nullifies message authentication entirely

highSeverityFindings.description: Contract initial state misconfigured by upgrade: committedRoot=bytes32(0) maps to a confirmed root e
Authorization bypass via zero-root: process() authorization logic defeatable by any caller using roo
Cross-function reentrancy: deposit() and withdraw() share mutable state
ERC-777 tokensToSend hook triggers reentrancy before transfer accounting
Classic reentrancy: external call made before state update in withdraw()
Flashloan-funded drain: attacker borrows to manipulate collateral ratio then liquidates
Oracle price manipulation via single-block TWAP window allows outsized borrow
Division-by-zero in share price calculation when totalSupply is drained
Sole-LP attack: only liquidity provider removes all LP then re-enters at will
Sandwich attack on large swap extracts MEV from retail users
